feed.title: Stories by David Motsoeneng on Medium
feed.description: Stories by David Motsoeneng on Medium

items.title: A Journey Through Enhancing Robot World
items.author: David Motsoeneng
items.description:
In the world of software development, iterative progress is not just a method — it’s a way of evolving and refining a project through a series of structured, focused improvements. Our recent experience with the Robot World project offers a valuable case study in how iterative development can enhance a complex software system. This blog will delve into the key aspects of our journey through Iterations 1 to 4, illustrating the process of refining our code base, implementing robust features, and achieving architectural excellence.

Iteration 1: Laying the Foundations

Goals:

Story and Scenario Development: The first iteration focused on establishing a solid foundation for our project by defining user stories and scenarios. This involved creating GitLab issues for each story and its associated scenarios, ensuring clear, actionable tasks for development.

Acceptance Testing: We set up acceptance tests against a reference server to validate our code’s functionality. This was critical for verifying that our implementations met the initial requirements.
- Build and Release: Implementing a Makefile was crucial for streamlining the build process, enabling tasks such as compiling code, running tests, and versioning software.

Key Achievements:

Defined stories like “Launch a Robot” and “Get the State of a Robot,” ensuring a clear understanding of the functionalities to be developed.
Established a systematic approach to build and release management, including tagging versions and packaging software for release.

Iteration 2: Expanding Functionality and Refining the Code base

Goals:

Enhanced Stories and Scenarios: The second iteration involved expanding on the initial stories with additional scenarios. For instance, we addressed scenarios for “Move Forward” and “Look,” incorporating edge cases and interaction complexities.
Code base Analysis: We revisited the code base to analyze improvements in code health and identified areas needing refactoring.
Docker Integration: Introducing Docker to our development process allowed us to containerize our application, ensuring consistency across different environments.

Key Achievements:

Developed comprehensive scenarios for advanced functionalities, such as handling obstacles in the world and managing robot launches.
Implemented Docker for local testing, which enhanced our development and deployment processes.

Iteration 3: Implementing Persistent Storage

Goals:

Database Integration: This iteration focused on adding database capabilities to our project. We developed features for saving and restoring the state of the Robot World, including managing multiple worlds.
Database Schema Design: We designed tables to store world data, ensuring that the database could handle different world configurations and maintain data integrity.
Data Access Patterns: Implemented Data Access Object (DAO) and Repository patterns to keep the domain code clean and maintain separation from database access logic.

Key Achievements:

Successfully integrated a database for persistent storage, allowing us to save and restore worlds with different configurations.
Enhanced code maintainability by applying best practices in database access and separation of concerns.

Iteration 4: Refining Architecture and Implementing a Web API

Goals:

Architectural Refinement: We revisited the architecture to ensure a clean separation between domain code and other system concerns. This included verifying that threading and console command handling were independent of domain logic.
Web API Development: Created a Web API layer to handle HTTP requests, such as retrieving and managing world data through RESTful endpoints.
Object-Relational Mapping (ORM): Replaced direct JDBC calls with an ORM layer, improving the abstraction of database interactions and simplifying future modifications.

Key Achievements:

Achieved a more modular and maintainable architecture by separating domain logic from other system components.
Successfully implemented a Web API for interacting with Robot World, enabling external access to world data and robot commands.
Refined data access by introducing ORM, which streamlined database operations and improved code quality.

Conclusion

Our journey through these iterations has not only enhanced the Robot World project but also demonstrated the power of iterative development in software engineering. By methodically addressing each goal, from defining initial functionalities to refining the architecture and integrating persistent storage, we’ve built a robust and flexible system.

Each iteration brought us closer to our vision, underscoring the importance of systematic improvements and the value of a well-defined development process. As we continue to evolve Robot World, these lessons will guide our approach, ensuring that our software remains adaptable and high-quality.
